symptom: After disabling Windows Error Reporting, WER becomes enabled again after no user action has been taken on systems added to Azure Security Center (Defender for Cloud).
rootCause: The Azure Watson Crash Listener component of Azure Security Center re-enables Windows Error Reporting to fulfill Azure Security Center logging and error reporting for added systems.
solution: Disable the crash collection component of the OMS Agent/Microsoft Monitoring Agent. Create a DWORD value under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\HealthService\Parameters named "Disable CDR Agent" with a value other than 0. Rely on Geneva Monitoring Agent or Azure Watson Agent for crash collection instead.

symptom: Customers receive unexpectedly high Azure bill and need detailed breakdown of Azure Log Analytics data usage beyond what billing provides.
rootCause: The Log Analytics Usage table in the Azure Log Analytics blade has a default retention period of only 1 month, making it impossible to review data from 2-3 months ago for billing analysis.
solution: Execute KQL queries in the customer Log Analytics workspace to get detailed information about ingested data. The KB provides specific queries for granular billing analysis beyond the default Azure billing portal data.

symptom: After onboarding Windows 7 SP1, Windows 8.1, Windows Server 2008 R2 SP1, Windows Server 2012 R2, or Windows Server 2016 to Microsoft Defender ATP, error 10103 is logged in the Operations Manager event log during HealthService startup: "In PerfDataSource, could not resolve counter instance Process, Processor Time, pmfexe. Module will not be unloaded. Workflow name: Microsoft.Windows.WindowsDefenderATP".
rootCause: The performance module in the management pack logic cannot find the perf counter for the sensor process (MsSenseS.exe) because the sensor process is not yet running when the event is logged.
solution: This warning event can be safely ignored. Once the MsSenseS.exe sensor process is initialized and running, the HealthService will locate the relevant performance counters as expected.

symptom: After onboarding a machine to Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (MDE), a 3rd party or custom application that writes to a single file on a shared network location experiences intermittent errors, crashes, or SHARING VIOLATION when writing to its network file.
rootCause: The application opens files with ShareMode: None. When MDE MsSense.exe opens the file for read (with ShareMode: Read, Write, Delete), the next time the application tries to open the file with ShareMode: None, it fails with a sharing violation error.
solution: Option 1 (preferred): Modify the application to open files with shareMode: Read and add retry logic. Option 2: Escalate to Defender for Endpoint engineering via ICM (aka.ms/m365dicm), providing a recording using MDEClientAnalyzer.cmd -c.

symptom: After isolating a Windows 10 machine from the network using MDE machine isolation feature, Windows Defender Application Guard (WDAG) instances are still able to reach the Internet, even though normal browser sessions are blocked.
rootCause: By design, WDAG instances are considered a separate entity and are not affected by machine isolation when enforced on the host machine via MDE platform.
solution: This is by-design behavior. WDAG instances run in isolated containers with reduced risk. Carefully review Application Guard policy settings when implementing this feature.

symptom: PowerShell cmdlets Get-ProcessMitigation and Set-ProcessMitigation fail with "Requested registry access is not allowed" (SecurityException) after installing October 2020 update KB4577671.
rootCause: The MsSense.exe key under HKLM Image File Execution Options has very restrictive permissions that do not allow administrators to read the key, causing access denied when cmdlets enumerate the registry.
solution: The Defender team has changed the permissions for the MsSense.exe key to be less restrictive. Install the servicing update when available.

symptom: RDP connections to Windows Server 2019 Core servers are immediately dropped or cannot be established after onboarding to Microsoft Defender for Endpoint. A black image is briefly displayed, then the connection terminates when Winlogon prematurely exits. GUI versions of WS 2019 are not affected.
rootCause: A race condition in ConsoleLogon.dll between the StartUI thread and UIThreadHostStartThreadProc thread causes LogonUI.exe to fail. UIThreadHostStartThreadProc calls GetThreadDesktop using the ID of the StartUI thread which has not yet converted to a GUI thread (NULL Win32Thread).
solution: Offboard from Defender Endpoint on Windows Server 2019 Core RDP Server, or deploy a GUI WS 2019 RDP Server. Install corrective OS updates (KB 5005102 for 1809/Server 2019, August 2021 8C update).

symptom: Abstract    This article describes 0xA IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL and 0x139 KERNEL_SECURITY_CHECK_FAILURE bugchecks in MpKslDrv caused by code regression in Defender Signatures prior to 1.339.305.0. introduced by Commit 751152e3.   The Defender signature definition that triggered the issue is 1.339.195.0 released on 5/8/2021. A fix was implemented in definition 1.339.305.0 released on 5/9/2021. Given that Defender automatically falls back on the basic/older driver (for older OSes) if a crash is detected, impacted devices appear to encounter this error once before rolling back to an older, unaffected driver.
rootCause: From Amitrajit Banerjee (ANTI-MALWARE):The issue is caused by an incompatibility between a newer version of the kernel support library driver used by Defender and older OSes (pre 19H1). Defender has two separate drivers (one for older OSes and one for current (post 19H1)) however, an error in the targeting logic was causing the new driver to be leveraged Server 2016/2019 which is what caused this spike. The said error was corrected yesterday morning (the targeting logic is part of our signature updates). As a side-note Defender automatically falls back on the basic/older driver (for older OSes) if a crash is detected.
solution: Update engine and signature versions. Engine version must be 1.1.18100.6, Signature version must be 1.339.225.0 or later. Use Get-MpComputerStatus to check. Clear downloaded signature cache with MpCmdRun.exe -removedefinitions -dynamicsignatures, then update definitions with MpCmdRun.exe -SignatureUpdate or Update-MpSignature.

symptom: Reported Symptoms Task manager hangs switching between tabs System slowness RDP logon to the machine took over 1 hour Running apps would take a long time, for example: Start > Run > Msconfig > ~10 seconds before the app would show up    Reported on Windows Server 2016
rootCause: Code defect in the Windows Defender Antimalware engine
solution: Bug is being tracked. Fix is tentatively scheduled for release on Jun 10, 2021.

symptom: Reported symptoms:System hangReported onWindows Server 2019
rootCause: Code defect in Deadlock in MpDlpShouldCheckMappedFileOperation on pre-create due to double lock on MpDlpData->SensitiveSectionFilesLock
solution: Defender fix is scheduled for release at the end of July 2021.

symptom: Since the 18th of January, full scans are consuming between 90 to 100% CPU while the CPU Throttling is configured to 50 (or in a case even 20). The CPU consumption is caused by the process MsMpEng.exe. So it seems the CPU Throttling isn't honoured anymore and the machine isn't usable anymore because of the CPU consumption.
rootCause: On the 18th of January the engine has been updated and the way worker threads are used during a full scan was modified. Unfortunately this had as effect to not honour the CPU Throttling generating a 100% CPU consumption.
solution: Workaround: Open the registry and navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender\MpEngine. Add the Key MpFolderScanThreadCount. Change the value of the Default REG_SZ to 2 (for a 4-core machine) or 4 (for a 8-core machine). Open a CMD as Admin and execute: setx MP_FOLDERSCAN_THREAD_COUNT 2 /M. Reboot the machine. Resolution: Fixed in the new engine release.

symptom: Applications that rely on Windows Task Scheduler operations may hang intermittently. The issue only occurs on Operating Systems that have Windows Defender Antivirus enabled and that are running engine version 1.1.15700.X (Windows 10, Windows Server 2016, Windows Server 2019).
rootCause: An intermittent issue in Windows Defender Antivirus engine version 1.1.15700.X may cause an application to hang if this application relies on the Windows Task Scheduler to execute operations.
solution: A Security Intelligence update fixes the issue. Ensure Windows Defender Antivirus has installed Security intelligence version 1.287.94.0 or higher. Follow guidance at https://support.microsoft.com/en-us to update the Security intelligence.

symptom: Logon hang, system hang, Outlook hang, Windows Search hang on systems with Windows Defender enabled.
rootCause: The Defender kernel driver wdfilter.sys should never block I/O operations from MsMpEng.exe. wdfilter.sys checks whether the current process is MsMpEng.exe before blocking. When a 3rd party kernel driver (e.g., CyProtectDrv64.sys) attaches a thread to another process, PsGetCurrentProcess returns wrong process, causing wdfilter.sys to incorrectly block MsMpEng.exe I/O, leading to deadlock.
solution: Fix released with Microsoft Common AntiMalware Platform (MOCAMP) 2019.03 update on March 20th 2019. Workaround: Remove the 3rd party driver (e.g., CyProtectDrv64.sys) that triggers the bad behavior.

symptom: When connecting external media (e.g. USB drive) encrypted using BitLocker: Windows Explorer hangs, Outlook hangs. Any application that does IO picked up by Windows Defender will hang.
rootCause: A Windows Defender thread handling incoming calls from Wdfilter driver does IO (e.g. OpenFile) and the open request ends up needing an IO manager lock owned by a thread running in a different process. The thread that owns the IO lock then takes action picked up by Windows Defender, causing deadlock.
solution: Issue addressed in engine version 1.1.15757.0, available on March 20th 2019.

symptom: On multiple Hyper-V servers, MpSigStub.exe version is reported as outdated and vulnerable by Tenable. After updating via MpCmdRun.exe -removedefinitions and -SignatureUpdate, MpSigStub is upgraded to 1.1.16500.1 but automatically downgrades back to 1.1.15400.2 after a day or two.
rootCause: Old Definition Update still present on the WSUS server due to corrupted sync, which caused it to not be superseded by newer Definition Updates. The primary source for Definition updates is WSUS, and older Definition Updates were being reinstalled.
solution: Check the WU.log to identify old Definition Updates being installed. Clean up the corrupted supersedence chain on the WSUS server to ensure newer definitions properly supersede older ones.

symptom: On a Windows 10 machine with Windows Firewall service turned on and Cisco NAM using both user and machine authentication, RDP connections from other machines disconnect.
rootCause: When the user logs in for user auth, an interface change event is triggered for the adapter. This triggers Windows Firewall quarantine filters which block network communication to the machine, causing RDP to fail.
solution: Issue being investigated by both Cisco and Microsoft PG (as of April 2020) under Bug ID 18058576.

symptom: Windows Defender 4.18.1902 for Windows 10 1909 deletes registry keys under HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies after receiving XML endpoint config file for Windows Defender from Config Mgr. This occurs with the default version of Defender from the Windows 10 1909 ISO.
rootCause: The particular version of Windows Defender (4.18.1902) was known to have issues with GPO handling (Bug 23403716).
solution: Update Windows Defender to a newer version before installing the Config Mgr agent. Use PowerShell commands: Update-MPSignature, Get-MPComputerStatus. These can be incorporated into task sequences. Alternative: install Windows 10 1909, update fully including Defender, capture the OS, then use that capture for imaging.

symptom: AntiVirusProduct and AntiSpywareProduct WMI classes (root\SecurityCenter2) used to get anti-virus and anti-spyware product details may not work since Windows 10 1709 (RS3). Running wmic /namespace:\root\SecurityCenter2 PATH AntiVirusProduct GET /value returns no data.
rootCause: AntiVirusProduct and AntiSpywareProduct WMI classes are not supported. Microsoft never made any public documentation on these classes.
solution: Use the IWSCProductList interface instead of the unsupported WMI classes.

symptom: Deploying AppLocker Policies on Windows 10 via WDAC/CM with policies set to Audit Mode causes Bugcheck 0x6D SESSION1_INITIALIZATION_FAILED. Memory dump shows AppLocker event 8018/8004 with SMSS.EXE prevented from running.
rootCause: Code defect in AppLocker policy merge. EXE and DLL rulecollections were not set properly, resulting in enforcing instead of auditing, leading to bugchecks. Bug 29232845: Merged AppLocker policies have missing properties after set-applockerpolicy.
solution: Recover affected machines by disabling AppId.sys driver offline using WinRE/WinPE: set HKLM\SYSTEM\ControlSet00X\Services\AppId\Start from 0 to 4. Apply fixes: 20H1/20H2 KB4598291, 1903/1909 KB4598298, 1809 KB4598296.

symptom: With WDAC (Windows Defender Application Control) enforced, the WSUS service and console fails to start on Windows Server 2016. Error in application log: Could not load file or assembly in temporary ASP.NET files directory. Organization used Device Guard to block this app.
rootCause: Windows Server 2016 does not support some features when creating WDAC rules based on directories.
solution: Upgrade the WSUS server to Server 2022, which allows more robust features for creating WDAC rules based on directories. Then configure a WDAC rule for the .NET temporary directory to restore WSUS functionality.

symptom: Microsoft Defender Antivirus MAPS is failing, causing MDE to not report properly. Client analyzer logs show AVCloudErr: Test connection to the Windows Defender Antivirus cloud service failed.
rootCause: AuthRoot local CRL repository configuration issue and firewall blocking Azure ATP access for system processes.
solution: Remove the previous key for AuthRoot local CRL repos. Ensure impaired servers have firewall access to Azure ATP for the system part. Verify connectivity using Defender ATP cloud service check.

symptom: MsSense.exe process on MDE-onboarded Windows machine experiences unexpected performance issues: abnormal memory consumption or constant abnormal CPU usage.
rootCause: MsSense.exe is a reactive sensor that collects OS signals. If the system has a lot of activity, it needs more resources to track all activity. The sensor is designed for minimal impact, so significant degradation warrants investigation.
solution: Collect a memory dump of MsSense.exe using ppldump.ps1 script: Rename ppldump.ps1.txt to ppldump.ps1, open PowerShell as admin, run Set-ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted, then execute the script. Escalate to MDE product group with the dump.

symptom: MsSense.exe on MDE-onboarded Windows machine causes AppCompat issues: locking files during build processes, other processes hit Sharing Violation or Access Denied when performing operations on files while MsSense.exe holds a lock.
rootCause: MsSense.exe is a reactive sensor that inspects and tracks all activity. It may hold a lock on a file while another process tries to delete or modify it, causing rare AppCompat issues.
solution: Open CMD as admin and run: reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Advanced Threat Protection" /v GroupIds /d OplockReadFix /f. Wait ~5 minutes for the change to apply, then test if the issue persists. This changes MsSense.exe file locking behavior to use oplock reads.

symptom: md4ws.msi fails to install on Windows Server 2012 R2. MSI log shows serviceconfig.cpp errors for WinDefend service configuration with AntimalwareLight launch type.
rootCause: Remnants of older Defender installation were not allowing the new md4ws.msi to install successfully.
solution: Enable MSI verbose logging: reg add HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v Debug /t REG_DWORD /d 7 /f and reg add .../Installer /v Logging /t REG_SZ /d voicewarmupx /f. Then install with: Msiexec /I md4ws.msi /l*vx c:	emperbose.log. Clean up remnants of old installation before reinstalling.

symptom: After upgrading Windows Defender for Endpoint platform to 4.18.2207.x, explorer.exe crashes when copying/pasting files. Requires DLP enabled in Defender and ForcePoint DLP installed (QIPCAP64.dll).
rootCause: Running two or more DLP applications (Defender MpDetours.dll and Forcepoint QIPCAP64.dll) on the same system causes a race condition where both try patching the same Windows function simultaneously, generating invalid code and stack corruption that crashes Explorer.
solution: Avoid running multiple DLP applications on the same system. Configure whether Defender DLP is enabled through the Compliance Portal. Bug 44925142 tracks the detour conflict fix.

symptom: Bugcheck code 0x50 PAGE_FAULT_IN_NONPAGED_AREA when using Windows Defender for Endpoint DLP and accessing a file that has a NULL or corrupt security descriptor.
rootCause: WdFilter.sys passes a null-pointer to _stricmp when encountering a NULL/corrupt security descriptor on a file. Root cause suspected to be NULL/corrupt security descriptor on the file.
solution: Workarounds: 1) Fix the security descriptor of the file to not be NULL. 2) Delete the offending file. If workarounds are not viable, engage MDE support or file ICM with "Microsoft Defender For Endpoint - PG Support" queue.

symptom: Windows co-managed devices with Endpoint Security workload migrated to Pilot Intune or Intune do not process or apply any Endpoint Security policies (Antivirus policies). Even after syncing with Intune, policies are not delivered.
rootCause: SCCM workload setup, dedicated collection configuration, collection availability for Endpoint Security policies, and group membership all need to be properly configured. Device Configuration Workload must also be set to Pilot Intune alongside Endpoint Security.
solution: Ensure SCCM Workload is set to Intune or Pilot Intune. Configure a dedicated Collection. Verify Collection is checked for Endpoint Security Policies in Endpoint Manager. Confirm device group membership. Set Device Configuration Workload to Pilot Intune alongside Endpoint Security.

symptom: Multiple WDAC blockage banners inform that a current Windows Defender Application Control policy has prevented third party executions. Customer is unaware of having released any WDAC policy. CodeIntegrity events appear in event log.
rootCause: Defender for Endpoint "Restrict App Execution" security countermeasure is enabled. This ATP-specific policy uses reserved GUID {4E61C68C-97F6-430B-9CD7-9B1004706770} and only allows Microsoft-signed files to run.
solution: Check in Windows Defender portal: Devices blade > select device > click 3 dots > check if "Restrict App Execution" is enabled. If enabled and the threat has been mitigated, disable/remove the "Restrict App Execution" feature.

symptom: In MDE portal, several Windows Server 2016 devices report failed Windows Defender Antivirus cloud service connectivity. MpCmdRun.exe connectivity test also fails.
rootCause: Network Service profile registry hive (HKU/S-1-5-20) was missing. MpCmdRun.exe ran as Local System instead of Network Service. Local System token has WinDefend SID causing traffic to match WSH Default Outbound block filter.
solution: Reinstall the OS to bring the Network Service profile registry hive back.

symptom: Windows Server experiences desktop heap exhaustion when manually onboarding devices to Defender for Endpoint using cmd scripts incorrectly.
solution: Follow official documentation for automating onboarding of individual devices.

symptom: High CPU on Domain Controller after enabling Microsoft Defender and gMSAs for Identity, millions of event 4662 logged. Disabling service resolves high CPU.
rootCause: MDI does not require Read all properties permission but excessive auditing of object access events causes high CPU.
solution: Clear selection for List contents, Read all properties, Read permissions on Descendant User/Group/Computer/msDS-GroupManagedServiceAccount/msDS-ManagedServiceAccount Objects.

symptom: System restore on Windows 10 does not revert Defender version to restore point state. Reverts to original OS setup version instead. Only works on RS4.
rootCause: GUID-named folder under Definition Updates fails to restore during system restoration.
solution: Known bug fixed in RS4 but persists in 22H2. Verify pattern and open EE task with PG.

symptom: Downloaded files from browser (especially Edge) are blocked. User must right-click > Properties > Unlock to use.
rootCause: Windows Defender PUA (Block potentially unwanted apps) feature is enabled.
solution: Disable PUA: Windows Security > App and browser control > Reputation-based protection > turn off Block downloads for Potentially unwanted app.

symptom: Windows Server 2022 crashing with stop 0x1E or 0x3B with return from pagefault 0x300180001.
rootCause: Cause within WdFilter!MpDlpSetEaOnDestination, under investigation.
solution: Workaround: MpCmdRun.exe -RevertPlatform, MpCmdRun.exe -RemoveDefinitions -Engine, then reboot.

symptom: ConfigMgr Endpoint Protection clients not applying policy. EPAMPolicy.xml fails with error 0x80004005.
rootCause: gpt.ini file has invalid GUIDs or incorrect formatting.
solution: Rename gpt.ini file and rerun ConfigSecurityPolicy.exe. May need reboot.

symptom: MDE reports High severity alerts that ShellExperienceHost.exe wrote to SAM registry hive on behalf of inactive user.
rootCause: False positive: ShellExperienceHost.exe runs under Low IL and cannot access SAM hive. Event is misattributed.
solution: Validate process path/CRC and Microsoft digital signature. Alert is false positive.

symptom: Test-MdiReadiness.ps1 fails to return correct audit/powercfg settings. Error -1073741790 (access denied).
rootCause: Defender ASR rule Block process creations from PSExec/WMI (d1e49aac-8f56-4280-b9ba-993a6d77406c) enabled in Block mode.
solution: Engage Defender Antivirus support via SAPs: Security/Microsoft Defender > ASR.
